title: See it their way
description: Understanding someone isn't the same as agreeing — and it's a superpower.
summaryPoints: Step into their shoes to understand the why — not to agree or excuse
Catch the trap: you judge them by their behavior, yourself by your intentions
Assume there's a reason you can't see — a bad day, fear, pressure, a need
Ask "what might be going on for them?" — curiosity beats judgment every time
You can hold your own view AND theirs at once — that's how you get unstuck

content.title: Same scene, two stories
content.body:
Your friend reads your text and doesn't reply for hours. You're already writing the story: they're mad, they're flaky, they don't care.

But you don't actually know. Maybe their phone died. Maybe they're swamped. Maybe they're sitting with bad news.

**Perspective-taking** is stepping into someone's shoes to understand why they might feel or act the way they do. Same scene — but now you've got more than one story for it.
content.imageAlt: Two heads connected by a bridge, sharing a perspective

content.title: Understanding ≠ agreeing
content.body:
Big myth to clear up first: getting where someone's coming from is **not** the same as agreeing with them. It's not excusing them either.

You can fully understand why your sibling snapped — they bombed an exam — and still think snapping wasn't okay.

Understanding is just information. It tells you what you're actually dealing with. What you do with it is a totally separate choice.

content.prompt: To take someone's perspective, you have to agree with them.
content.explanation: Understanding why someone feels or acts a certain way isn't the same as agreeing or excusing it — it's just information you can use.

content.prompt: What's the most understanding read here?
content.context: A new kid in your group barely talks and skips the hangouts. Some people have decided they're stuck-up.
options: They might be shy or nervous in a group that already knows each other
They clearly think they're better than everyone
They obviously don't want to be friends, so drop it
They're being rude on purpose to get attention
content.explanation: Quiet usually means nervous, not snobby. Reaching for the kinder, more likely reason is what perspective-taking looks like in action.

content.title: The blame trick your brain plays
content.body:
Here's a glitch we all run. When someone messes up, we blame who they are: "he's so rude," "she's careless."

When **we** mess up, we blame the situation: "I was just stressed," "traffic made me late."

Same behavior, two completely different explanations — and we always give ourselves the generous one. Psychologists call it the **fundamental attribution error**. Spotting it is step one to seeing fairer.
content.imageAlt: A lit match touching a fuse, like a quick judgment catching

content.instructions: One word flips this backwards. Tap it.
content.sentence: The fair move is to judge others as harshly as we judge ourselves.
content.explanation: We excuse ourselves but judge others by their behavior — the fix is to give them the same benefit of the doubt.

items.text: "He's just a jerk, end of story."
"I wonder if something happened to her today."
"What might be going on for them right now?"
"Maybe he's under pressure I can't see."
"They did that on purpose to annoy me."

content.title: Flip the script
content.body:
Once you know the trick, you can flip it. Assume the other person — just like you — is probably reacting to something you can't see: a rough day, fear, pressure, some need that isn't being met.

Two questions do the heavy lifting:
- **"What might be going on for them?"**
- **"What would I feel in their spot?"**

You're not making excuses. You're getting curious instead of jumping to the worst story.

content.prompt: Each behavior might have a hidden reason. Match a likely one.

pairs.right: Got overwhelmed and ran out of energy

pairs.left: Went quiet in the group chat
pairs.right: Going through a rough patch privately

pairs.left: Was short and blunt with you
pairs.right: Rushing and didn't mean it that way

content.prompt: The question that opens the door: "What might be going ___ for them?"
content.explanation: "What might be going on for them?" swaps a verdict for curiosity — and curiosity is what actually defuses things.

content.title: Two kinds of empathy
content.body:
Empathy comes in two flavors, and you want a mix:
- **Cognitive empathy** — getting their point of view in your head
- **Emotional empathy** — feeling a bit of what they feel

Get their view, feel a little with them, and conflict starts to cool. People who feel understood get less defensive, and trust grows.

The best part: you can hold your own view AND theirs at the same time. It's not either-or.

content.prompt: Put the see-it-their-way move in order.
steps: Notice the judgment you've already jumped to
Get curious instead — there's probably more going on
Imagine how you'd feel standing in their spot
Check it: ask, or stay open to being wrong
content.explanation: Catch the snap judgment, swap it for curiosity, picture their side, then actually check — that's the loop that gets you unstuck from "I'm just right."

content.prompt: What does the curious move sound like?
content.context: Your teammate didn't do their part of a group project and you're annoyed.
options: "You're so lazy, you never pull your weight."
"Hey, is everything okay? You usually come through on this."
Say nothing and just do it all yourself, quietly fuming
Tell the rest of the group they're useless
content.explanation: Leading with a real question assumes a reason and keeps the door open — way more likely to fix the actual problem than an attack.

content.prompt: Read the other person's feeling — where does each one sit?

items.text: A friend who's furious you forgot their birthday
A classmate buzzing because they aced the test
A sibling who's flat and withdrawn after bad news
A roommate calm and at ease after sorting a problem

content.explanation: Naming where someone else's feeling sits — by energy and pleasantness — is empathy in action. You're reading their weather before you respond.

content.title: Their side
content.intro: A friend snaps at you out of nowhere in the hallway. You feel the sting. Where you go next decides how this lands.

steps.situation: They bite your head off over something tiny. Your first move?

options.text: Pause and wonder what might be going on for them
options.feedback: Curiosity first. You bought yourself a second instead of reacting on autopilot.

options.text: Snap right back — they started it
options.feedback: Now you're both lit up. Matching the heat just escalates it.

options.text: Decide they're just a rude person and write them off
options.feedback: That's the attribution error — blaming who they are instead of what's happening to them.

steps.situation: You remember they had a big exam this morning. What now?

options.text: Think "not my problem" and stay annoyed
options.feedback: Understanding their stress doesn't excuse the snap, but it does cool yours.

options.text: Assume there's a reason you can't see and stay open
options.feedback: Exactly — you're reaching for the likely explanation instead of the worst one.

options.text: Tell the group chat what a jerk they were
options.feedback: Venting the worst story to everyone locks it in and burns the bridge.

steps.situation: You catch them later, still a bit tense. You say…

options.text: "You were so out of line earlier."
options.feedback: Leading with the verdict puts them straight on the defensive.

options.text: Give them the cold shoulder so they get the message
options.feedback: Silent punishment leaves you both guessing and fixes nothing.

options.text: "Hey, you seemed really off earlier — you okay?"
options.feedback: A genuine question. You named what you saw and left room for their side.

steps.situation: They admit they were stressed and apologize. You…

options.text: Tell them you get it — and that the snap still stung a bit
options.feedback: You held both: their reason AND your honest feeling. That's the whole skill.

options.text: Pretend it was totally fine to avoid the awkwardness
options.feedback: Understanding them doesn't mean erasing your own feeling — you can hold both.

options.text: Make them grovel for a while to feel better
options.feedback: Punishing the apology trades trust for a tiny hit of being right.

content.outro: You didn't have to agree with the snap to understand it. Curiosity over judgment — that's how you stay close and stay honest at the same time.
